steps.title: Pin down Lady Howard's view
steps.content: Lady Howard says "it is time that [Evelina] should see something of the world" and warns that young people "too rigidly sequestered" develop romantic illusions. Her implied criticism of Villars is that he has sheltered Evelina too much and she needs broader experience.

steps.title: Match that view to the choices
steps.content: We need a choice that says Villars is over-protective and that more varied experience would help Evelina — nothing more specific than the text supports.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'shielding her from the influence of other young people': the text never narrows the issue to "other young people"; Lady Howard talks about the world generally. Overreach. ✗
  • Choice B — 'imparted to Evelina his own idealistic view': misreads the passage. Lady Howard says sequestered young people invent a paradise on their own, not that Villars taught it to her. ✗
  • Choice C — 'unduly wary of the Mirvans': nothing in the excerpt suggests Villars distrusts the Mirvans specifically. Unsupported. ✗
  • Choice D — 'overly protective... would likely benefit from a greater variety of experiences': this is exactly Lady Howard's point — Evelina has been kept too sheltered and should now see the world. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
